segments.s218-g121: برفتند (barftand) is past tense, 3rd person plural — 'they departed/they went.' وز here is the archaic contracted sequence و + ز (and + from), functioning as a connective 'and from' linking the two actions; it is not the modern noun senses listed. جای (jây) is the noun 'place' and takes the postpositional sense 'from (their) place' governed by ز. برخاستند (barkhâstand) is also past 3rd plural 'they rose/up' or 'they stood up,' so the clause literally says 'they departed and (from) their place rose,' i.e., they stood up and left. Word order and the two successive verbs produce a tight sequence of actions.
segments.s218-g122: همه شب (hame-ye shab) is an adverbial phrase 'the whole night' or 'all night.' همی (hamī) is the classical progressive/habitual particle placed before the verb to indicate continuous or repeated action ('kept/was'); here it marks duration/continuity. لشکر (lashkar) is the object 'army/host,' and آراستند (ârâstand) is past 3rd plural of آراستن meaning 'they arranged/arrayed/prepared' — so together: 'all night long they were arranging the army.'
segments.s218-g1: چو here at the start is temporal 'when'. شد is the past of شدن 'to become' and takes the following nominal phrase as its complement: 'روی گیتی' = 'the surface (face) of the world'. The second چو is comparative 'like/as' linking 'روی گیتی' to the simile 'دریای قیر' (a sea of tar). Word order is Verb (شد) following the temporal marker and preceding the subject-complement phrase typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s218-g2: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. Here ناهید is the first item (proper name), followed by the predicate پیدا 'visible' (پیدا functions as a predicative adjective meaning 'apparent/visible'). The second conjunct 'بهرام و تیر' is coordinated by و. The whole segment negates the visibility of these named figures.
segments.s218-g3: بیامد is the past tense 'came'. Here دمان appears immediately after the verb and functions as a name or identifier of the arriving person (treated as a proper name in context); دیده‌بان is the noun 'sentinel/watchman' and پیش طوس means 'before/in the presence of Tūs' (پیش = in the presence of, طوس = the hero Tūs). The verb-front order (بیامد دمان) is a poetic arrangement meaning 'Daman came'.
segments.s218-g4: دوان (here adverbial/participial) means 'running' or 'rushingly' modifying the arrival; و links to the next clause. شده (past participle of شدن) + روی 'face' = 'his face became', and چون + سندروس is the simile 'like sandalwood/fragrant wood'. The clause thus describes his appearance as changing while he ran in.
segments.s218-g5: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying گفت which here is the speech verb 'said' (the supplied sense list is lexical; the intended use is the verb meaning 'to say'). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone, and پهلوان سپاه is the addressee 'champion of the host' (پهلوان = champion/hero, سپاه = army/host). The whole segment is a formulaic speech opening: 'Thus he said, "O champion of the host..."'.
segments.s218-g6: از ایران سپاه آمد is a normal prepositional phrase 'an army came from Iran' (از = from, ایران = Iran, سپاه = army) with آمد 'came' as the verb. The following از نزد شاه means 'from the presence of the king' (از = from, نزد = in the presence of, شاه = king); repetition of از marks two different sources/origins (geographical origin and origin in the court).
segments.s218-g7: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a high commander). بخندید is past tense 'laughed' (in narrative past). با + مهتران means 'with the مهتران' — here مهتران are the senior officers/chiefs; the precise dictionary senses given for مهتران don't match the usual 'chiefs', so that lemma is treated as a title/group and is the object of the preposition با.
segments.s218-g8: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory formula (here introducing what the spahbed calls them: 'O ...'). نامداران is an adjective/noun meaning 'the renowned/illustrious ones', و is the conjunction 'and', and کنداوران is a coordinated noun ('کنداور' literally 'diggers/excavators' in the provided senses) used here as a descriptive epithet. The phrase is the content of the spahbed's laugh/salutation: 'O renowned ones and (the) کنداوران.'
segments.s218-g17: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. بر + چرخ گنبد is a prepositional phrase meaning 'upon the wheel/sky (the vaulted heavens)' where چرخ = 'the sky/heaven' and گنبد = 'dome/vault'; کشید is a poetic past tense verb 'drew/stretched' (here 'drew [its] dome over') — note the provided lemma senses for کشید are for a different root, so the intended literary meaning is 'to draw, stretch' rather than cultivation.
segments.s218-g18: شب تار شد is a short clause 'the night became dark' (تار used poetically for 'dark'); شد is the past of شدن 'became'. از جهان ناپدید is a prepositional phrase 'vanished from the world' with از = 'from', جهان = 'the world', ناپدید = 'vanished'. Note تَار as 'dark' is poetic/archaic and not present in the supplied senses list.
segments.s218-g19: یکی here = 'a certain / someone'. انجمن کرد is the verb phrase 'held an assembly' (انجمن = assembly, کرد = did/held). خاقان چین is the title + locator 'the Khagan of China' (noun phrase in apposition/subject position). Word order is typical poetic inversion: 'someone held an assembly — the Khagan of China.'
segments.s218-g20: بدیبا is an adverb 'marvellously / splendidly'. بیاراست is a past/performative form of the verb 'to adorn / array' (here 'adorned' or 'set in order'). روی زمین is a frozen locative phrase 'on the face/surface of the earth' (روی = 'upon/on', زمین = 'earth'). Together they mean he splendidly made the earth beautiful/arrayed it.
segments.s218-g21: بپیران is a prepositional dative 'to the elders' (ب + پیران). چنین گفت is 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = 'he said' — note the supplied senses list for گفت gives nouns like 'conversation' rather than the past-verb; here it functions as the verb 'said'). کامروز modifies the reported speech: 'this very day' and جنگ is the object 'war' (i.e. 'let us make war this day').
segments.s218-g22: بسازیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortative 'let us make / we should make' and here means 'let us undertake (war)'. و = 'and'. روزی here functions adverbially 'a single day / even one day' (the supplied senses don't list the noun 'day' so the match is missing). نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not / should not', and درنگ is the verbal noun 'delay'. Together: 'and we must not delay even a day.'
segments.s218-g23: یکی = 'a certain one' or 'someone'. با = 'with'. سرافراز گردنکشان is a descriptive phrase: سرافراز 'lofty / triumphant' modifies گردنکشان, a poetic plural 'the neck‑raisers' meaning 'the proud ones' or 'rebels'. The whole phrase means 'someone together with his triumphant proud men.'
segments.s218-g24: خنیده is a participial/past form of خنیدن 'to sing / chant' (here attributive: 'having sung' or 'who chanted'). سواران = 'horsemen / riders'. دشمن کشان is a compound epithet 'enemy‑slayers' or 'those who strike the enemy' (دشمن = enemy; کشان is a deverbal/adjectival form meaning 'pulling/striking/slaying' in poetic usage). The phrase groups the mounted warriors as active enemy‑slayers; note some supplied senses do not include these exact poetic meanings, so a few supplied entries do not match perfectly.
segments.s218-g41: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the following noun phrase. افسر here means a helmet or crest (noun head) and is followed by the genitive modifier سر پیلبان ('the head of the mahout/elephant‑driver'); پرنگار is a post‑nominal adjective meaning 'richly ornamented' that modifies افسر. The whole phrase is an absolute/descriptive noun phrase (no finite verb in this segment) setting up an image.
segments.s218-g42: This is a verbless descriptive clause where همه 'all' is the subject/pronoun, پاک 'clean/neat' is a predicate adjective, and the preposition با ('with') introduces the attendant ornaments طوق (collar/necklace) and گوشوار (earring). The coordinating و simply links the two 'با' phrases; the clause relies on the context to supply the copula 'were'.
segments.s218-g43: هوا شد is a simple past/perfective pattern meaning 'the air became' or 'the air was (so)'; ز introduces the cause ('because of'/'from') and بس functions adverbially ('so much' / 'so many'). پرنیانی (an adjective) modifies درفش (banner/standard), so the phrase means 'because of so many silken standards.'
segments.s218-g44: چو here is the simile 'like/as', introducing a comparison: 'like a market of folds.' بازار چین is a genitival phrase where چین ('folds') follows بازار, i.e. 'a market of folds' (an image of many colorful folds). The following adjectives سرخ, زرد, بنفش are simple coordinate color adjectives describing those folds; the repeated و merely links them.
segments.s218-g45: سپاهی is the plural noun 'soldiers/army.' برفت here is the past tense verb 'went forth/marched' (classical form of رفت); اندرَان is a locative/genitive adverb meaning 'among them' or 'into that (place)'; دشت رزم is a noun phrase 'plain of battle' (دشت 'plain' modified by رزم 'battle'). Note: the provided senses list for the verb token does not match this usage, so its sense is marked accordingly.
segments.s218-g46: کزیشان is a compound referring back to the group ('from/of them' — کز = 'from which/of which' + ایشان 'them'). همی is the archaic/progressive particle here emphasizing ongoing or habitual aspect ('still/indeed'). آرزو is the noun 'desire/wish' and خواست is the past tense verb 'wanted/desired'; بزم (direct object) means 'feast/banquet.' The clause therefore means 'some among them still desired a feast.'
segments.s218-g47: زمین شد is 'the earth/ground became'; بکردار is an adjective used predicatively to qualify زمین — here best understood as 'patterned/marked' or 'well‑appointed' (the exact classical sense isn't in the supplied list), and چشم خروس is a simile 'like the eye of a rooster' (چشم + noun). The adjective follows the copula as is typical in Persian descriptions.
segments.s218-g48: ز introduces the cause ('because of'); بس functions adverbially ('so much' / 'so many'). The coordinated list رنگ و آرایش و پیل و کوس names the elements causing the effect: 'color and ornament and elephants and (war‑)drums' — the repeated و simply links the nouns.
segments.s218-g73: بپیران is a prepositional/target phrase meaning 'to the elders' placed before the verb for emphasis; چنین is an adverb 'thus' modifying گفت; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used in the simple past); خاقان چین is the subject noun phrase 'the Khaghan of China' (چین is a genitive/attributive place-name modifying خاقان). The word order is topical (indirect object/adverb before verb) typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s218-g74: که here is a complementizer introducing the reported question/clause ('that' / 'asking whether'); اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'; چه is the interrogative 'what'; سازیم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive form of ساختن/ساخت (here idiomatically 'to do/bring about' = 'shall we do'); بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; دشت is 'plain'; کین is a genitive noun 'vengeance/hatred', so بر دشتِ کین = 'on the plain of vengeance'.
segments.s218-g75: ورا is the pronominal prepositional form 'to him' (indirect object) placed before the verb; گفت is the past 'said'; پیران is the subject 'the elders' (they who speak); کز (classical contraction of که ز) introduces the subordinate clause meaning 'that/from which' or 'since/that'. اندک سپاه is the noun phrase 'a small army' that the clause will comment on.
segments.s218-g76: نگیرند is the negative present/subjunctive/optative 3rd-plural of گرفتن (here 'to take/receive' used idiomatically 'to gain' or 'to be remembered'); یاد means 'memory/remembrance/fame' (object of گرفتن); اندرین is a demonstrative-locative 'in this' or 'on this'; رزمگاه is 'battlefield'. Together: 'they will not gain remembrance in this battlefield.'
segments.s218-g77: کشیدی is the 2nd-person-singular past tense of کشیدن used here in the sense 'you endured/suffered'; چنین is 'such/so'; رنج is 'suffering/hardship' (direct object); و is 'and'; راه دراز is the noun phrase 'a long road/journey' (راه = road, دراز = long). So the verb governs two coordinated objects.
segments.s218-g78: سپردی is the 2nd-person-singular past of سپردن 'you entrusted/handed over' (transitive, object omitted but understood); و is 'and'; دیدی is the 2nd-singular past of دیدن 'you saw'; نشیب و فراز is a paired, idiomatic noun phrase 'descent and ascent' = 'the lows and the highs' (nouns coordinated). The two verbs are parallel actions attributed to 'you.'
segments.s218-g79: بمان is a 2nd-person singular imperative/jussive 'stay/remain' (an instruction); تا with a numeral expresses duration here ('for' / 'for the space of'); سه روز = 'three days' (duration); اندرین is the locative demonstrative 'in this' or 'here in this'; رزمگاه is 'battlefield'. The clause gives a temporal/durational command.
segments.s218-g80: بباشیم is 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive 'let us be/remain' (a suggestion linked to the imperative); و is 'and'; آسوده is an adjective 'at peace/relieved'; گردد is the present-subjunctive/optative of گشتن used impersonally here 'become/will become'; سپاه is the subject 'the army' placed after the verb in classical order. The sense: 'Let us stay, and the army will become at rest.'
segments.s218-g33: This is a noun phrase whose head is سپاه (the host/army). سنانهای (pl. of سنان) 'spears' begins the phrase and is followed by two postpositive adjectives رخشان و جوشان ('gleaming/lustrous' and 'seething/fiery'), forming a genitival/possessive relationship: 'the spears of the host' or more idiomatically 'the host's spears'. Word order is typical classical/poetic Persian: noun + adjectives + possessor/head.
segments.s218-g34: شده is the past participle/compound verb meaning 'have become / have appeared' (here 'appeared/arrived'). روی کشور is a locative phrase 'on/upon the face/surface of the country' (روی used in the sense 'surface, face'). ز introduces an origin or source in poetic register ('from'), so ز لشکر سیاه means 'from the black host/army'. Together the clause says the spears/host have come to/appeared on the country's surface from that black army.
segments.s218-g35: This clause has the verb نهادند (past plural 'they placed/put') and a moved locative/agent phrase ز پیلان 'on/at the elephants' placed before the verb for poetic order. بر پنج زین is the direct object phrase 'on five saddles' (بر here marks 'on/upon' and زین = saddle). The verb's placement and omission of an explicit subject are normal in Persian narrative (subject implied as 'they').
segments.s218-g36: بیاراست is a past/preterite verb meaning 'adorned' or 'made splendid' (here passive/third-person 'was adorned' or 'they adorned'). دیگر functions as 'another/the other' and refers back to an additional item; it is followed by بدیبای (here the textual form appears irregular) and چین 'China' to indicate material/origin. The phrase means 'another was adorned with Chinese brocade' — the subject is again omitted and inferred.
segments.s218-g37: زبرجد (a precious stone) is fronted for emphasis, followed by نشانده, a verbal adjective/past participle 'set/placed'. بزین اندرون corresponds to بِ زین اندرون 'in/on the saddle inside' — بزین here is a contracted prepositional phrase ('on the saddle') and اندرون means 'within/inside'. So the clause reads 'a peridot was set inside the saddle.'
segments.s218-g38: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' indicating material or composition. دیبای زربفت is a compound noun 'gold-woven brocade' (دیبا = brocade/silk, زربفت = gold‑woven), and پیروزه‌گون is an adjectival compound 'turquoise‑colored'. The whole phrase describes the material/appearance: 'of gold-brocade, turquoise‑hued.'
segments.s218-g39: This is a condensed list: بزرین ('large/great') modifies the following nouns رکیب and جناغ پلنگ. رکیب here functions as a companion/associate term (in this martial/ornamental list it refers to a rider's fitting or accessory) and جناغ پلنگ literally 'leopard's breast' is a compound meaning a breast/pectoral decorated with a leopard motif; adjectives follow heads in poetic Persian and items are listed in parataxis.
segments.s218-g40: This continues the list: بزرین و سیمین are two adjectives 'large/great and silvery' modifying the plural nouns جرسها و زنگ 'bells and gongs' (جرسها is jرس + plural -ها). The construction uses simple coordination (و) and places adjectives before the nouns they modify for rhetorical effect — common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s218-g81: سپه را کنم زان سپس به دو نیم is a clause in which کنم (1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used here as a future/intention) takes سپه را as its direct object (را marks the definite object). زان سپس is a two‑word temporal/sequence phrase 'after that/thereupon', and به + نیم expresses direction/target 'into (two) halves' — literally 'I will make the army into two halves after that.'
segments.s218-g82: سرآمد here is used as 'conclusion/arrival' (the coming of something) rather than 'foremost'; کنون 'now' fixes the time, and روز پیکار و بیم is a noun phrase 'the day of battle and fear/dread.' Together the phrase states that the end/turn has come — 'now is the day of battle and dread.'
segments.s218-g83: بتازند is a 3rd‑person plural verb ('they rush/attack/ride') in an epic present tense implying repeated or imminent action; شبگیر is an adjective/noun 'night‑attacker/nocturnal (band)'; تا + نیمروز is a temporal clause 'until noon.' The clause means 'the nocturnal raiders ride/charge until noon.'
segments.s218-g84: نبرده is the negative past participle/perfect form of بردن ('not taken/has not taken') used to state an action not accomplished, and it links to the following noun phrase سواران گیتی‌فروز as its object/patient: 'not having taken/captured the world‑illuminating horsemen' (i.e. they remained unbeaten/uncaught by those riders).
segments.s218-g85: This is a nominal list of arms: بژوپین (an archaic/obscure term — here a weapon or piece of armour), و خنجر 'and dagger', بتیر is a fused form meaning 'with/by arrow' (با + تیر), and و کمان 'and bow'. The items are coordinated by و and form an instrumental/accumulative catalogue of weapons.
segments.s218-g86: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle 'still/continually'; رزم جویند is a compound verb phrase (رزم 'battle' + جویند 'they seek') in 3rd‑person plural meaning 'they seek battle/are eager for combat'; با بدگمان is an adverbial phrase 'with mistrust/suspicion' modifying how they act. So: 'they still seek battle with suspicion.'
segments.s218-g87: دگر نیمه روز دیگر گروه is a poetic piling of 'other/another' (دگر ... دیگر) with نیمه روز 'half of the day / the later part of the day' and گروه 'troop/band'. Read together it means 'in the next/other half of the day another troop' (i.e. a different group in the later part of the day).
segments.s218-g88: بکوشند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive of کوشیدن 'they strive/attempt'; تا introduces a temporal clause 'until'; شب برآید ز کوه uses برآید (present/subjunctive 'rises/appears') and ز 'from' to say 'until night rises from the mountain' (i.e. until nightfall comes up over the mountain).
segments.s218-g97: بسازیم is a 1st‑person‑plural hortative/subjunctive form ('let us make' / 'we shall make'); یکبار is an adverbial numeral meaning 'once' or 'one time' and here modifies the hortative; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; جنگ‌آوریم is a 1st‑person‑plural present/hortative built on جنگ‌آور used as a verbal idea 'be/begin belligerent, make war' = 'we go to war' (poetic economy turns what is nominal in modern Persian into a verbal sense). The whole clause is a two‑part hortative: 'let us (do X) and (do) Y.'
segments.s218-g98: The clause is verbal: بنیـه بر + pronouns/objects omitted for brevity. بریشان is the fused/poetic form of بر + ایشان meaning 'against/on them' (i.e. 'upon them'); در here is the noun 'door, gate' not the preposition; و is 'and'; کوه 'mountain'; تنگ 'narrow' modifies کوه; آوریم is 1st‑person‑plural subjunctive/hortative of آوردن 'to bring, drive'. Together: 'we will drive/bring them against gates and narrow mountain‑passes.'
segments.s218-g99: بایران is an adjectival/adverbial form meaning 'to/into a waste/desolate condition' (here 'desolate' / 'barren'); گذاریم is 1st‑person‑plural present/subjunctive of گذاشتن/گذاشتن in the hortative sense 'let us put/leave/lay' (here 'we will leave/make'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; ایدر is 'here' (classical form of این‌جا); سپاه is the noun 'army/host'. Word order is compact and somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'we will leave (them) a wasteland; from here the army...'.
segments.s218-g100: نمانیم is 1st‑person‑plural present/subjunctive of ماندن used impersonally/indefinitely here ('will not remain' / 'shall not be left'); the noun series تخت و نه تاج و نه شاه is coordinated with repeated نه ... نه to mean 'neither ... nor ...' (neither throne nor crown nor king). The verb appears before its coordinated objects for poetic inversion: 'there will remain neither throne, crown, nor king.'
segments.s218-g101: بر و بومشان is a fixed pair meaning 'their land and dwelling/home' (بر و بوم + possessive -شان). پاک is an adjective 'clean/pure' used here idiomatically as part of a paired result meaning 'made clean/purged' (i.e. emptied); و is 'and'; یران (here historically written for ویران) means 'ruined/laid waste' (this is an archaic/variant form of ویران); کنیم is 1st‑person‑plural hortative/subjunctive of کردن 'we will make/do'. The structure is 'we will make their land and home clean and ruined' = 'we will utterly devastate their land.'
segments.s218-g102: The clause contrasts two kinds of fighting with the adversative particle نه introducing the contrast: 'not (the) جنگِ یلان but (the) جنگِ شیران کنیم.' جنگ before a noun here is a noun ('a fight/war'); یلان is 'heroes' (yllān) and شیران literally 'lions' but used metaphorically for 'brave/ferocious men.' کنیم is again the hortative/optative 'we shall make/bring about.' The sense: 'it shall not be a heroes' war but a lions' battle.'
segments.s218-g103: This segment is a simple coordinated list of people: زن 'woman/women' + و 'and' + کودک 'child' modified by خرد 'small' (here functioning as an adjective, 'little child') + و پیر 'and the old/elderly' + و جوان 'and the young.' Adjectival modifier خرد follows the noun in this poetic sequence ('کودک خرد' = 'small child').
segments.s218-g104: The phrase uses repeated نه ... و نه ... to mean 'neither ... nor ...': نه شاه ('neither king') و کنارنگ (an obscure/classical term; likely a courtier, retainer, or marginal official but the exact meaning is uncertain) و نه پهلوان ('nor champion/hero'). The segment denies that any dignitary or hero will remain; note that کنارنگ is lexicalized here and its precise sense is uncertain in classical Persian.
segments.s218-g9: چو at the start is a temporal conjunction meaning “when/now that.” یار is a noun (here ‘ally/companion’). آمد is a past tense verb “came/arrived.” اکنون reinforces the temporal sense (“now/at present”). نسازیم is a first‑person plural present/subjunctive-like form of ساختن with the negative prefix ن- here functioning as a hortatory/precautionary negative: “let us not make/start.” جنگ is the direct object ‘war/battle.’ The clause therefore reads literally “When the ally came, now let us not make war.”
segments.s218-g10: گهی ... گه ... is a pair of adverbials meaning “at times / sometimes … and sometimes …,” showing alternation. با + شتاب(‑یم) uses با as an adverbial marker “with” and شتابیم is a present/optative form expressing ‘we hurry / we are hasty.’ و is the coordinator “and.” گه (second occurrence) repeats the adverbial “sometimes,” با marks the manner again, and درنگ is a noun meaning “delay/pausing.” Grammatically this is an adverbial sentence: “Sometimes we with haste, and sometimes with delay.”
segments.s218-g11: بنیروی is a prepositional/genitive compound meaning “by the power of.” It governs the following genitive noun یزدان ‘God.’ گو here is a poetic/postpositive element meaning “‑speaking/‑called” or an epithet attached to the name that follows (an attributive/clitic use common in classical Persian). پیلتن is a proper name/place that the epithet attaches to. Syntactically this whole phrase functions as an instrumental phrase: “by the power of God, (the) Pīltan...”
segments.s218-g12: بیاری here should be read as the older form b + یاری = به یاری “with the help/assistance” (the printed token is fused). بیاید is a jussive/optative present meaning “may (he) come” or “let (him) come.” بدین is the compound به + این “to/into this,” and انجمن is the destination noun “assembly/meeting.” Together: “(may he) come to this assembly with aid.”
segments.s218-g13: ازان is a fused demonstrative/prepositional adverb meaning “from that / thereupon.” دیده‌بان is the subject “the watchman/sentinel.” گشت is the past of گشتن used here in the sense “became/turned” (resultative/become). روشن‌روان is a compound adjective meaning “bright‑spirited/cheerful,” and follows as the predicate complement: “the watchman became cheerful.”
segments.s218-g14: همه is the universal subject marker “all/everyone.” مژده is the object “good news/tidings.” دادند is the past tense third‑person plural “they gave” (here idiomatically “they gave/shouted the tidings”). پیر و جوان is a paired nominal phrase “old and young,” functioning as an appositive/emphasis on who did the giving: “everyone, old and young.”
segments.s218-g15: طلایه is the noun “vanguard/forefront.” فرستاد is the simple past “(he) sent/dispatch(ed).” بر is the preposition “on/upon/onto” marking location or direction, followed by دشت “plain,” and جنگ specifies the plain as the “field of battle.” Word order here is V‑O with postpositional location: “(he) sent the vanguard onto the plain of war.”
segments.s218-g16: خروش is a noun “a roar/uproar.” آمد is the past “came” (used impersonally here: “a roar came”). از is the preposition “from,” کوه is the source “mountain.” و coordinates the next phrase; آوای is the construct form “the sound/voice of,” and زنگ is “bell/ringing/alarum” — together: “a roar came from the mountain and the ringing sound (of bells).”
segments.s218-g49: برفتند is the past (perfective) 3rd-person plural of بررفتن/رفتن (‘to go, depart’) and appears before its subject as is common in epic poetry. شاهان لشکر is a noun phrase 'the kings' host(s)' (شاهان = kings, لشکر = army/host), and ز جای is a prepositional phrase ('from their places/positions') with ز as the poetic preposition 'from' and جای = 'place/position'.
segments.s218-g50: هوا پر شد is a copular/resultative construction: هوا ('the air') + پر ('full (of)') + شد (past of شدن, 'became'), literally 'the air became full.' از introduces the cause/source ('with/ by'), and ناله کرنای is a genitive/adjunct phrase 'the wailing/cry of the karna (trumpet),' here expressed by juxtaposing nouns without an ezāfe as is normal in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s218-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' از دور ('from afar') is an adverbial phrase modifying بدید (past 'saw'). طوس سپهبد places the name and title together (apposition: 'Tous, the spahbed'), and بدید is the 3rd-person past form of دیدن used here as the main verb.
segments.s218-g52: سپاه is the subject 'the army.' آنچ بودش is a parenthetical/relative-like phrase: آنچ 'that which' + بودش (بود + enclitic -ش 'it/its') = 'what it was' or 'as it was,' qualifying رده (ranks/rows). برکشید is the past tense (here 3rd person) of برکشیدن 'to lift/raise' (i.e. to form or raise ranks). The sequence reads 'the army, as it was, raised its ranks.'
segments.s218-g53: ببستند is the past 3rd-person plural of بستن with the prefix ب- (they closed / drew together). گردان ایران is a compound noun phrase (گردان 'troop/division' + ایران 'of Iran') and میان is an adverb 'in the middle/center' placed after the object; overall: 'they closed the Iranian divisions in the center.'
segments.s218-g54: بیاورد is the past form of آوردن 'to bring' (here narrative past, rendered 'they/he brought'), followed by the object گیو (proper name). اختر کاویان is an appositive epithet placed after the name without an ezāfe—اختر 'star' used figuratively and کاویان 'of the Kayanians'—so 'Giv, the star of the Kayanians.'
segments.s218-g55: از ... تا is the standard 'from ... to ...' correlative construction: از آوردگه ('from the arrival/assembly-place' — آوردگه = آورد + گه, 'place of bringing/assembly') تا سر تیغ کوه ('to the top/summit of the mountain's blade'). تیغ کوه is a poetic juxtaposition 'blade (ridge) of the mountain'; سر marks the summit.
segments.s218-g56: سپه بود is a descriptive past clause 'there was an army' or 'the host was.' از ایران marks origin ('from Iran'), and the repeating phrase گروها گروه is a poetic pluralizing/ intensifying pattern meaning 'groups upon groups' or 'many groups' (first گروها is a plural form, repeated by گروه for emphasis).
segments.s218-g25: ببینیم is a 1st-person-plural optative/subjunctive meaning 'let us see' or 'we shall see' that introduces the following embedded clause. کایرانیان is the plural subject 'the Kayanian people' and برچیند is a 3rd-person (subjunctive/jussive) verb form meaning 'clear away / drive off / pluck up' — the embedded clause thus reads 'whether the Kayanian will clear (it)'.
segments.s218-g26: بدین here functions as the fused prepositional phrase 'به + این' (classical poetic form) meaning 'to/at this' or 'in this'; رزمگه اندرون = 'the battlefield, inside' with اندرون marking interior location. با کیند is an interrogative/elliptical sequence 'with whom (do they act)?' where کیند is an archaic 3rd-person plural form 'they do' (the main verb is left implicit because of the higher verb 'ببینیم' and the previous clause).
segments.s218-g27: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' fronting the sentence; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (note: the provided sense list did not include this verbal meaning); پیران is the subject 'the elders'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...', and خاقان چین is a noun phrase 'the khagan of China' with چین in postposition indicating 'of China'.
segments.s218-g28: خردمند is an adjectival predicate 'wise'. شاهیست is a contracted copular expression (شاهی + است) meaning 'is kingly / is a ruler'; با آفرین is a prepositional phrase literally 'with praise' functioning as an evaluative complement 'praiseworthy' or 'worthy of acclaim'. Word order places these predicates after the noun introduced by که.
segments.s218-g29: بران is an archaic locative/adverb 'thither / thereupon'; رفت is the past of 'to go'; باید following the verb expresses necessity ('must / ought to'). The clause continues with که introducing a reason: او را هواست is a predicative construction where را marks او as the object and هواست (هوا + ست) means 'there is a desire for him / he is beloved' — literally 'he (object) is desired'.
segments.s218-g30: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. رای تو is 'your counsel/opinion' (رای = plan or counsel here); بر ما همه is a locative/benefactive phrase 'over/among all of us' with بر meaning 'upon/over' and همه 'all'. پادشاست is a copular predicate (پادشاه است contracted) meaning 'is king/sovereign' — i.e. 'your counsel rules us all.'
segments.s218-g31: وزان پس is a formulaic sequence 'and from that / and then'; برآمد is the past 'arose / issued forth'. ز + پرده‌سرای is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the curtain-hall / from the pavilion' with ز as the prefixed poetic 'from' and پرده‌سرای the place 'pavilion/curtain-room'.
segments.s218-g32: خروشیدن here is used as a noun/masdar 'the roaring/sounding' (the infinitive/gerund sense) and takes کوس ('war-drum') as its theme: 'the sounding of the drum'. با introduces the accompaniment 'with' and کرنای is 'trumpet / horn' — so the phrase means 'the roaring/sounding of drums with trumpets'.
segments.s218-g57: چو is the clause-introducing particle here meaning "when" (temporal). The following three nouns are a coordinated list of proper names/titles (کاموس ، منشور ، خاقان چین) with the final one head-marked by چین "of China"; word order is list → modifier, so "the Khāqān of China." No verb in this segment; the temporal clause sets up the following action.
segments.s218-g58: Another چو begins a parallel temporal clause: چو + verb. بیورد is a past/preterite form "brought" (he/it brought). و is the simple coordinator "and." چون here functions as a comparative/likeness "like/as," introducing شنگل (an obscure name/term) qualified by بافرین (an adjective/adverb meaning "praiseworthy/with praise"). The two چو-clauses are paralleled to give two aspects of the arrival.
segments.s218-g59: نظاره is a noun "gaze/sight." بکوه is the locative phrase "to/at the mountain" (به + کوه contracted), and هماون (همان) "that very/that same" points back to a particular mountain. شدند is the 3rd-person plural past of شدن "became/turned" — idiomatically here "their gaze turned/fixed on that mountain." Word order is topical noun + locative + predicate.
segments.s218-g60: نه here negates the following clause ("they did not..."). بر آرزو is an adverbial phrase "by/wish/according to desire" (آرزو = desire; بر marks the relation), پیش دشمن شدند = "they came/advanced before the enemy" (پیش = before/in front of; شدند = became/came). Together the clause means they did not, willingly or from desire, go forward to face the enemy.
segments.s218-g61: چو = "when" (temporal). از دور is a prepositional phrase "from afar/from a distance." خاقان چین = "the Khāqān of China" (title + place), and بنگرید is the past-form of the verb نگریستن "to look/gaze" (here rendered as "looked" or "looked out"). The whole is a temporal clause: "When the Khāqān of China looked from afar..."
segments.s218-g62: خروش is a noun "cry/roar." سواران ایران is a genitive phrase "the riders/horsemen of Iran" (سواران = horsemen/riders; ایران = Iran/Iranians). شنید is the past of شنیدن "he heard." Word order is object (the cry of the riders) before the verb, a common classical/poetic ordering.
segments.s218-g63: پسند آمدش is an impersonal/experiential idiom meaning "it pleased him" (پسند = liking; آمدش = 'came to him' = 'he found it pleasing'). گفت here introduces reported speech ("he said..."). کاینت (کاین + ت) is the demonstrative "this/that which" pointing to what he is about to describe, and سپاه is the noun "army/host." The structure is: "It pleased him; he said, 'This host...'"
segments.s218-g64: This is a nominal list describing the army: سوران (an ethnic/name term here) followed by adjectives/epithets رزم‌آور "warlike/warrior" and کینه‌خواه "vengeful/avenger." The conjunction و links the last two qualities. Word order is noun (group name) → descriptive epithets.
segments.s218-g89: This phrase is a noun phrase + object marker followed by a verbal infinitive: 'شب تیره' = 'dark night' (adjective after noun, poetic order). 'آسودگان را' is the definite object ('the rested/peaceful ones' + object marker را). 'بجنگ' here functions as an infinitival/directional complement meaning 'to battle' or 'into battle' and attaches to the following verb 'برم'.
segments.s218-g90: برم is a 1st-person verb form (poetic/older Persian) meaning 'I will bring/go' (here 'I will bring'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). بریشان is بر + ایشان = 'upon/against them'. شود is the present/subjunctive of شدن meaning 'become' or 'come to be'. کار تنگ is a nominal phrase 'affair/work (becomes) narrow' — an idiom meaning their situation is made difficult.
segments.s218-g91: نمانم is a 1st-person negative verb 'I will not remain / I will not stay'. که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/so that' or 'for'). آرام گیرند is a light-verb construction: آرام (calm/rest) + گیرند (they take/receive) = 'they take rest / become calm'; here it is a present/subjunctive form. هیچ negates the subordinate clause ('no one / none / at all').
segments.s218-g92: This is a simple noun phrase giving accompaniment/possession: 'سواران من' = 'my horsemen' (possessive order common in poetry), 'با' = 'with' indicating accompaniment, and 'سپاه و بسیچ' = 'army and many (a host)'. The conjunction و links سپاه and بسیچ; بسیچ here functions as a noun meaning 'a great number / multitude.'
segments.s218-g93: بدو is the prepositional pronominal form 'to him/unto him' (introducing an indirect object). گفت is the past verb 'said' (3rd person). کاموس is a proper name (Kâmūs) and is the subject of گفت. کین رای نیست is a short clause: کین = 'vengeance/vengeful', رای = 'counsel/opinion/plan', and نیست = 'is not'; together 'vengeance is not a (good) counsel' or 'this is not counsel of vengeance.'
segments.s218-g94: بدین مولش اندر مرا جای نیست breaks down as: 'بدین' = 'with/under this' (demonstrative/adv. of manner), 'مولش' = 'its/his master' (مول + -ش), 'اندر' = 'in/within', 'مرا' = 'me' (object), and 'جای نیست' = 'there is no place' (جای = place, نیست = is not). The whole clause means 'under/with such a master there is no place for me' (i.e. I cannot remain under such a lord).
segments.s218-g95: This line repeats the demonstrative 'بدین' ('with this/by this') plus 'مایه' = 'resource/substance': 'بدین مایه مردم' = 'with this resource (the) people...' The second 'بدین گونه' = 'in this way/manner' and 'جنگ' = 'fight/war' completes the thought: 'with these means the people fight in this manner.' Word order is poetic and somewhat elliptical.
segments.s218-g96: This is a rhetorical question: 'چه باید' = 'what is needed / what use is it' (چه often introduces an exclamatory or interrogative). 'بدین گونه' = 'in this manner', and 'چندین درنگ' = 'so many delays' (چندین = many/several, درنگ = delay). The clause asks why so much delay is necessary given the manner/means just described.
segments.s218-g113: This is a short verbless phrase with the finite verb inserted: تلی کشته بینی = 'you will see a heap of the slain'. بینی is the 2nd-person singular present/future form of دیدن used here as 'you see/will see'. The locative phrase ببالای کوه ('on the mountaintop') follows the verb in the line for metrical order, giving the place where the heap is seen.
segments.s218-g114: This nominal clause lacks an overt action verb and relies on the previous clause's sense; it reads as a temporal statement: 'You (تو) — tomorrow (فردا) — from/of (ز) a detachment (گردان) of Iran (ایران) — a band/troop (گروه)'. In context it's understood as 'tomorrow you will have/bring a troop of the Iranian host'; ز functions as the partitive/relational preposition 'from/of'.
segments.s218-g115: بدانسان که introduces a result/way clause: 'in that manner, namely that…'. که is the subordinating conjunction 'that', and ایرانیان سربسر means 'the Iranians, all around / everywhere' (سربسر = throughout/everywhere). The phrase sets up what follows as the consequence for the Iranian people.
segments.s218-g116: ازین پی here means 'along/from this track/after this event' (از + این + پی), where ین is an orthographic element representing 'این' and پی functions as 'path/track'. نبینند is 3rd-person plural present/optative negative of دیدن — 'they will not see' (or 'they shall not see'). جز introduces the exception 'except' and مویه‌گر names the exception class 'mourners'.
segments.s218-g117: بدو is the dative/clitic 'to him'; گفت here introduces speech (the past action 'said'), and خاقان is the speaker 'the khaghan'. جزین رای نیست is an independent clause: جزین = 'except/other than this', رای = 'counsel/plan', and نیست negates it — 'there is no counsel except this.' Note the poetic word order (dative + verb + subject).
segments.s218-g118: بگیتی 'in the world' (a fixed locative), چو 'as/like', تو 'you' — this sets a comparison: 'there is no one in the world like you…'. لشکر آرای is a classical compound: لشکر (army) + آرای (someone who arrays/arranges/adorns) — 'one who arrays an army'. نیست closes the clause: 'does not exist'. The phrase praises the addressee's unmatched military skill.
segments.s218-g119: همه نامدارن = 'all the renowned (men)'. بدین هم‌سخن is a frozen phrase meaning 'of this same opinion / agreed to this' (ب + این = 'to this', هم‌سخن = 'of one speech / like-minded'). The clause declares unanimous agreement among the notables.
segments.s218-g120: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. کاموس شیراوژن is a personal name in subject position. افگند is the past tense of افکندن 'to throw/cast' (here 'cast down' or 'overturned'), and بن is the object meaning 'root/foundation/base'. Together: 'that Kamus Shirāvožan cast down the foundation.'
segments.s218-g105: نمانم is the negative form of ماندن with the first‑person singular ending -م; here it functions as a jussive/performative 'let not remain / I will not leave'. The sequence بر و بوم و جای is a coordinated list of locative nouns ('land / home / place') with و as the conjunction; word order (noun + و + noun ...) is the normal coordination pattern. بایران (an adjectival/epithet here) qualitatively describes the following nouns as belonging to or concerning Iran (poetic usage of بایر/بایران to mark the country/its lands).
segments.s218-g106: نه ... و نه ... is the stock negating repetition (neither ... nor ...). Each noun (کاخ, ایوان, چارپای) is itemized as things that will not remain; the verb نمانم from the previous segment scopes over this negated list. چارپای is a compound meaning a four‑footed animal, here 'beast of burden'.
segments.s218-g107: ببد is an archaic/past form of بودن ('it was' / 'there was') introducing the time span. روز چندین = 'several days' (noun + quantifier). چه باید گذاشت is a rhetorical question: چه (what) + باید (modal 'must / can') + گذاشت (here 'to spend / pass (time)') — collectively meaning 'what could one do (what was there to spend) / how could one spend (those days)?' and it links as a lead‑in to the consequence in the next segment.
segments.s218-g108: This is a simple nominal list (غم و درد و تیمار) followed by داشت, the past of داشتن used here as a predicative 'had / experienced'. بیهوده qualifies تیمار ('care') and applies to the whole list: they possessed 'grief, pain, and useless care'. Word order: theme (nouns) + past verb.
segments.s218-g109: یک امشب = 'just one night / this single night' (یک intensifies امشب). گشاده is an adjective 'wide, open' modifying the command; مدارید is a present/imperative (2nd‑person plural) jussive meaning 'be lenient / allow / open' and here takes راه ('the road / way') as its object: 'open the way wide (for tonight)'.
segments.s218-g110: که introduces a purposive/subordinate clause ('so that / so they may ...'). ایشان 'they' is the subject, and برانند is the present subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person plural of راندن meaning 'drive away / send off' (here 'may they be driven away' or 'let them drive off'). زین رزمگاه is an archaic locative phrase 'from this battlefield' (زین = 'from this' in older usage); رزمگاه = 'battlefield.'
segments.s218-g111: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. باد سپیده دمان is a poetic genitive phrase 'the wind of the dawn' (سپیده 'dawn', دمان 'hem/skirt/edge' in poetic use). بردمد is an archaic/past verb (here used poetically) from بردن meaning literally 'carried / bore off' but here idiomatically 'rose / began to blow' — the whole clause sets the time ('when the dawn wind arose').
segments.s218-g112: سپه جمله = 'the whole army' (سپه 'army' + جمله 'all, entire'). باید is the impersonal modal 'must / it is necessary', and که introduces the complement clause; اندر 'in/into' marks direction/placement. چمد here denotes a mount‑/saddle‑related receptacle (they must get into their saddles), but that particular sense is archaic/poetic and not among the provided lemma senses, so its precise lexical option is marked as not listed.
segments.s218-g65: سپهدار (a title) is placed before پیران (here functioning as a name or epithet) — a common classical order 'title + name'. دگرگونه is an adverb 'differently/otherwise' and گفت is the simple past 'said'; together دگرگونه گفت = 'said otherwise.'
segments.s218-g66: هنرهای مردان is a genitive noun phrase 'the feats/skills of men' (plural marker هنرهای + possessor مردان). نشاید is an impersonal, negative modal ('it is not fitting' / 'one should not'), and نهفت is the verbal stem/participle 'to be hidden/concealed' used in poetic diction after the modal to yield 'should not be hidden.'
segments.s218-g67: This is a relative clause: سپهدار + کو (classical/poetic relative particle here functioning like 'who') introduces 'who…'. چاه is 'pit/well' and پوشد (3rd pers. singular, older/poetic form) means 'covers/conceals'; بخار is the instrument/thing 'with steam/vapor.'
segments.s218-g68: برو here functions adverbially/elliptically ('then/thereupon' or literally 'go') and introduces the following action. اسپ is the subject 'the steed/horse' and تازد is the 3rd-person (poetic) form 'rushes/gallops'; بروز شکار is an adverbial phrase 'on the day/occasion of the hunt.'
segments.s218-g69: ازان به که is an idiomatic comparative clause 'from that (it is) better that…' where ازان sets up the comparison, به introduces the comparative, and که introduces the following clause. بر is the preposition 'on/upon,' خیره modifies روز ('glaring/open/striking day' in poetic usage), and نبرد is 'battle' — 'on the glaring/open day of battle.'
segments.s218-g70: هنرهای دشمن is 'the enemy's arts/skills.' کند is the 3rd-person verbal form 'makes/puts' and with the following phrase زیر گرد ('under dust') forms a figurative result: 'puts (them) under dust' = 'buries/overwhelms/defeats' (a poetic way to say routs or tramples).
segments.s218-g71: ندیدم is the 1st-person past negative 'I did not see / I have not seen' and takes the following coordinated direct objects سواران و گردنکشان ('horsemen and proud ones / rebels'). The coordination is direct: 'I have not seen X and Y.'
segments.s218-g72: بگردی is a relative/optative sense 'who would turn / who, turning' (classical/poetic verbal form) that links back to سواران و گردنکشان. مردانگی is the abstract noun 'manliness/valor' and زین نشان is a compact phrase 'from/by this mark/token' (زین here is classical/poetic; نشان = 'sign/token'). Together the phrase qualifies the kind of valor: 'valor shown by this token.'

sentences.s218-s61: They departed and rose from their places; all night long they kept arranging the army.
sentences.s218-s1: When the face of the world became like a sea of tar, neither Nahid was visible nor Bahram nor Tir.
sentences.s218-s2: The sentinel Daman came before Tus, running, and his face became like sandalwood.
sentences.s218-s3: Thus he said, 'O champion of the host, an army from Iran has come from the presence of the king.'
sentences.s218-s4: The spahbed laughed with the chiefs and said, 'O renowned ones and excavators.'
sentences.s218-s9: When the sun drew its dome over the sky-wheeled vault, the night grew dark and vanished from the world.
sentences.s218-s10: A certain Khagan of China convened an assembly and magnificently adorned the face of the earth.
sentences.s218-s11: He said thus to the elders: 'This very day let us make war, and we must not delay a single day.'
sentences.s218-s12: A certain one, with the proud head‑raisers, (and) the horsemen — the enemy‑slayers — sang / chanted.
sentences.s218-s21: From the richly ornamented crest on the mahout's head; all (were) neat, wearing collars and earrings.
sentences.s218-s22: The air was filled, from so many silken standards, like a market of folds — red, yellow, and purple.
sentences.s218-s23: Soldiers marched into that plain of battle; from among them some still desired a feast.
sentences.s218-s24: The ground became patterned like a rooster's eye because of so much color and ornament, elephants and war‑drums.
sentences.s218-s37: The Khaghan of China said to the elders: "What shall we do now on the plain of vengeance?"
sentences.s218-s38: The elders said to him that with so small an army one will not win remembrance on this battlefield.
sentences.s218-s39: You have endured such hardship and a long road; you entrusted (it) and have seen descent and ascent.
sentences.s218-s40: Stay here for three days in this battlefield; let us remain, and the army will be at rest.
sentences.s218-s17: The spears, gleaming and seething, of the host appeared upon the face of the country from the black army.
sentences.s218-s18: They set five saddles upon the elephants; another was adorned with Chinese brocade.
sentences.s218-s19: A peridot was set within the saddle; it was of gold‑woven brocade, turquoise‑hued.
sentences.s218-s20: Large stirrups/straps and a leopard‑breast ornament — large and silvery were the bells and gongs.
sentences.s218-s41: Then I will split the army in two; now the day of battle and dread has come.
sentences.s218-s42: The night‑raiders will press on until noon; they have not been taken by the world‑brightening horsemen.
sentences.s218-s43: With cuirass(?) and dagger, by arrow and bow — they still seek battle with mistrust.
sentences.s218-s44: In the other half of the day another troop will strive until night rises from the mountain.
sentences.s218-s49: Let us strike once and make war; we will drive them against gates and narrow mountain-passes.
sentences.s218-s50: We will leave them desolate; from here no army will remain — no throne, no crown, no king.
sentences.s218-s51: We will lay waste their land and home; it will not be the warriors' fight but the lions' battle.
sentences.s218-s52: Women and little children, the old and the young — neither king nor courtier (obscure term) nor champion.
sentences.s218-s5: Now that the ally has come, let us not make war; sometimes we act with haste and sometimes with delay.
sentences.s218-s6: By the power of God may Pīltan come to this assembly with aid.
sentences.s218-s7: From there the watchman became bright‑spirited; everyone — old and young — gave the good tidings.
sentences.s218-s8: He sent the vanguard onto the plain of battle; a roar came from the mountain and the ringing sound of bells.
sentences.s218-s25: The kings' hosts departed from their places; the air filled with the trumpets' wailing.
sentences.s218-s26: When Tous the spahbed saw (from) afar, the army, as it had been arranged, raised its ranks.
sentences.s218-s27: They closed the Iranian divisions in the center; they brought forth Giv, the Kayanian's star.
sentences.s218-s28: From the muster-place to the mountain's ridge, the army of Iran stood in group after group.
sentences.s218-s13: Let us see whether the Kayanian men will clear (the field), and with whom they will be inside this battlefield.
sentences.s218-s14: Thus the elders said that the khagan of China is a wise, praiseworthy ruler.
sentences.s218-s15: He must go thither; he longs for him, for your counsel is sovereign over all of us.
sentences.s218-s16: And then from the curtain-hall there rose the sounding of drums and the blare of trumpets.
sentences.s218-s29: When Kâmus and Manšur and the Khāqān of China came, and when he brought (it/them) — like Shengel, in glory.
sentences.s218-s30: Their gaze was fixed on that very mountain; they did not, of their own will, advance before the enemy.
sentences.s218-s31: When the Khāqān of China looked from afar, he heard the cry of Iran's horsemen.
sentences.s218-s32: It pleased him; he said, 'This host — Sūrān, warlike and revenge-seeking.'
sentences.s218-s45: I will bring a dark night against the peaceful ones into battle, so that their affairs become strained.
sentences.s218-s46: I will not remain (idle) so that any take rest — my horsemen, with the army and a great host.
sentences.s218-s47: Kâmūs said to him: 'Vengeful counsel is not [right]; with such a master there is no place for me.'
sentences.s218-s48: With such resources the people fight in such a manner — what need is there for so many delays?
sentences.s218-s57: You will see a heap of the slain on the mountaintop; tomorrow you will have a troop from the Iranian host.
sentences.s218-s58: So that the Iranians everywhere will see along this path nothing but mourners.
sentences.s218-s59: To him the khaghan said, 'There is no counsel other than this; in the world there is no one who arrays an army like you.'
sentences.s218-s60: All the famed men were of this same mind: that Kamus Shirāvožan cast down the foundation.
sentences.s218-s53: I will leave nothing in Iran — no village, dwelling, or place; no palace, no iwan, no beast of burden.
sentences.s218-s54: Several days passed — what could one do but endure? — they had grief, pain, and futile care.
sentences.s218-s55: Just this one night, open the way wide, so that they may be driven away from this battlefield.
sentences.s218-s56: When the dawn wind rose, the whole army must needs mount into the saddles.

sentences.s218-s34: The commander who covers a pit with steam — then the steed gallops off on the day of the hunt.
sentences.s218-s35: It is better, then, that on the open day of battle the enemy's skills be driven under the dust.
sentences.s218-s36: I have seen no horsemen or proud rebels who, turning, show such valor as this token.
